mnemonics.title: Mutex Types Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: I ICE - Inconsistent Effects, Competing Needs, Interference, Inverse - helps remember all four mutex types

mnemonics.title: GraphPlan Two Phases
mnemonics.content: E-S: Expand first, then Search - E for Expansion, S for Solution extraction

mnemonics.title: Propositional vs Action Levels
mnemonics.content: P for Past/Previous state (literals), A for Action (what can be done now)

mnemonics.title: Leveling Off Failure
mnemonics.content: LEVEL = Last state before solution - when graph stops growing and goals missing, no solution

acronyms.usage: Dummy action that maintains literals across levels without changes
Represents literals that could be true at step i of planning
Contains actions applicable at step i with preconditions satisfied by Pi-1
Relation indicating two elements cannot coexist in same plan step
Algorithm using Planning Graphs for efficient plan finding

visualTips.visualization: Picture a ladder with alternating rungs: odd rungs = propositions (what's true), even rungs = actions (what can do). The ladder builds upward as planning progresses.
Think of mutex as a 'forbidden sign' or chain linking two elements that cannot shake hands - they conflict and cannot be in the same plan step.
Imagine working backwards from a destination (goal) to starting point (initial state), picking actions that lead to where you came from.
Think of NOOP actions as a conveyor belt that carries unchanged literals forward to the next level, like carrying water without spilling.
Picture the graph 'flattening out' like a pond becoming still - no new ripples (literals) can form, indicating maximum exploration reached.

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant menu: propositional levels are the 'tables available' (what's true), action levels are 'dishes you can order' (actions you can take).
Like students who can't work together - one wants the projector on, another wants it off (Inconsistent Effects); one needs the computer, other needs it (Competing Needs).
Like finding your way home by retracing your steps backwards from a landmark until you recognize your starting point.
Like walking while holding a glass of water - you don't need to do anything to keep the water, it just persists (unless you spill = another action deletes it).
Like exploring a maze level by level - each expansion reveals more of the maze until you find the exit (goal) or realize you've covered everything.
